Just saw this movie…this is how I’ll describe it. 
Into the Spiderverse 
Is our generations Back to the future trilogy. The first is a Masterpiece of storytelling is fun for the whole family. 
Across the Spiderverse is our part 2. It’s an excellent middle chapter that leaves you stunned and not wanting it to be over. I’m sure when part 3 comes out part 2 will make a lot more sense. 
Like Back to the future; We didn’t really need sequels to into the Spiderverse cause that movie was a masterpiece, but we did and this part 2 was a fun, slightly darker, and more emotional middle half. I can’t wait for part 3 to see how this all ends. 
I have good faith in the sequel. This trilogy is going to encapsulate the best of our part in pop culture. These movies are the best representation of how far we’ve come as a society. They’re inclusive, and creative. They focused more on making a great superhero movie first, they made the characters real and lovable. Beautifully acted and directed and these movies are amazing to see on the big screen.
The only bad part about this movie was that it had to end. Amazing job guys, you totally blew my mind with the animation on this one, can't wait for part two!

It’s honestly hard for me to judge it and felt a bit underwhelming because it very much felt like 1/3 of a longer story and a not very good way.

I completely understand why it had to be this way; 

I always remember reading up on all the cut content in the first film, namely Gwen and miles romance getting more focus, and I really respect them for cutting it as to keep the movie from being to bloated.

It was a dark and stormy night in New York City. The rain was coming down in sheets, and the wind was howling. The perfect night for a villain to strike.

And that's exactly what Electro did.

Electro, also known as Maxwell Dillon, is a powerful villain who can control electricity. He used his powers to cause a blackout across the city.

Spider-Man was alerted to the blackout and quickly swung into action. He found Electro at the power plant, where he was wreaking havoc.

Electro was a formidable opponent. He was able to easily outmaneuver Spider-Man, and he even managed to electrocute him.

Spider-Man was in trouble. He was weakened by the electricity, and he was starting to lose consciousness.

But just when it seemed like all hope was lost, Spider-Man had an idea. He knew that Electro's powers were based on electricity. So, he used his webs to create a giant electromagnet.

The electromagnet attracted Electro's electricity, and it began to overload him. Electro screamed in pain as he was zapped by his own power.

Finally, Electro could take no more. He exploded in a blinding flash of light.

The city was saved, and Spider-Man had once again triumphed over evil.

But Electro was not gone for good. He would return, and next time, he would be even more powerful.

Spider-Man knew that he would need to be ready for Electro's next attack. He would need to train harder and become even more skilled if he was to stop Electro from destroying the city.

But for now, Spider-Man could rest easy. He had saved the city, and that was all that mattered.

Miguel has always been my favourite Spidey, but I love that he's the villain in this (trailer at least, we'll see about the whole movie), because Miguel is an absolute asshole, especially at the beginning.  The world of Marvel 2099 is an extremely harsh, unforgiving and ruthless place, and Miguel got to his spot pre-Spider-powers near the top of Alchemax by being just as cold and ruthless as those around him.

Learning how to be Spider-Man mellowed him *a lot*, and returned his empathy for others and the necessity of looking out for more than just yourself. But he's still some of an asshole, and he's still arrogant, because he *is* the smartest person in the room, almost no matter where he is. He's certainly smarter than 616 Peter, MCU Peter, Miles, Peter B and likely all the other Spider-Men collected in these scenes. 

But 616, MCU and Peter B, plus Miles all have something that Miguel is still lacking somewhat: Wisdom. 

Mind you, I'm pretty sure that Miguel has one power that none of the other Spiders do: A venomous bite. Not nearly as cool or useful as Miles' shocks, let alone his disappearing trick, but  it's still a uniqueness for Miguel alone. 🕷🕸

Okay, Miguel is very insistent on keeping Miles at bay. Exclaiming that Miles has no idea what he’s doing when he’s trying do whatever it is he’s trying to do, maybe save his father? Or perhaps his world? I’m thinking Morlun is going to be involved, and Miles’ potential recklessness in revealing Miguel’s Spider-Haven is what’s driving Miguel to become unhinged in trying to fight Miles. It reminds me a bit of Loki, where He Who Remains states that if he dies, ‘something far worse’ will come in his stead. Similarly, if Miles does what Miguel is trying to prevent him from doing, it risks revealing everyone to Morlun. This might be based on Miles potentially being reckless with the multiverse? Even though IIRC the end of the first movie wasn’t his fault. That’s the only justification I can think of for Miguel’s mindset, especially when he berates Strange and Tom!Peter, who are also reckless with the multiverse and may have risked revealing themselves to Morlun. So bringing him along might have been a dangerous risk.

And considering this was originally slated as a Part 1 and 2, just like Infinity War…

Obviously all of this is speculation, but it’s my best guesstimate.

Personal theory right here:

I don’t think it’s miles trying to prevent his equivalent of uncle Ben dying, but rather it’s a consequence to Spider-Man’s actions as a hero, when being Spider-Man, you tend to lose things personal to you as a result of being a hero.

Spider Gwen loses her friend in return of saving the city,

Peter’s relationship is tested because of his responsibility to protect the city,

And now miles is going to lose his dad because he’s going out on a mission to take down the spot.

If we put it like this, miles is trying to break that cycle because he believes if he has the ability to do it, he can do both things. Whereas Miguel is against it because it goes against being Spider-Man, you have to sacrifice the things close to you, including people in order to save the world, and in this case, the multiverse.

It’s a similar question that the green goblins asks of Peter, during that scene where he gets Peter to either save one or lose the other, the people or his loved one. And Peter answers with both.

It’s also a response to how people want miles to be, whereas he believes he can do his own thing as Spider-Man.

Something tells me he might not be fully correct, since he might have to live with the fact that maybe you can’t do everything. And that there are things you can’t do. But I’m curious how this will play out since it seems Miguel might have a more malicious intent than we might realise,

Not in a supervillain sense, but more that from what we know in the last trailer, he had a daughter, and potentially losing that, has driven him to be miserable and determined to ensure that his ideals as Spider-Man are right, that sacrifices are necessary, because if Miles saves his father, that means he was wrong and maybe it wasn’t a choice to sacrifice his own child, but that he couldn’t save her even if he tried.
Didn't his Uncle Aaron die

@absolution6191 he did, but that’s not what I was getting at, his uncle is the catalyst for Miles’ call, but his dad is the result of his responsibilities as Spider-Man, being Spider-Man, you would have trouble trying to maintain a healthy work life balance because you’re basically sacrificing your time and energy to help the city no matter the case. Hence why I listed examples of what happened to Peter and Gwen when they became spider people.

Honestly, it's not only awful, but also, it's the worst 'Spider-Man' film ever made. I can't phantom the love the first had. It completely destroys the character by stripping the identity away, in exchange for diversity nonsense, and reducing Peter to an idea. 

Spider-Man has never been an idea, he's always been a character and that film literally misunderstood what the character was meant to be. Not only, does it say: you can be unoriginal, and you can be unoriginal, but you can copy and paste and steal <said character> name. Indivuality is destroyed in the name of banality; acceptance of lazy writers wanting to take an established character and take the easy route. It's a sick perversion of the times of today. It's garbage. It's not this joyous celebration, it's a cynical, and as much as I hate the word, woke, door masked as creative, fun and inspiring. The Spider-Verse itself is such lazy writing. Not only too does it not match the nature of Spider-Man, but it also ruins the dichotomy, duality and spirituality of Peter by stripping him of his home and self. 

I think The Incredibles (2004) predicted this trend firmly by saying: "When everyone is super, no one will be."

I'll break it down simply: When everyone is Spider-Man - no one is Spider-Man.

@naxxheart I don’t really agree with your opinion.

The first film is great, and just skimming through some of your critiques shows they aren’t really that valid.
You conflate Peter being an idea and Spider-Man being an idea. Peter isn’t an idea, he’s a character, very distinct from any other character who is Spider-Man. Miles and Peter are two very different people and have different struggles and different arcs, Miles is not a rehash of Peter.

And you act as though having diverse spider people is a new thing and is done to push diversity. Stan Lee said himself that one of the best design char of Spider-Man is his shit covers his entire body so anyone could imagine themselves being Spider-Man.

And I suppose you could say the film is cynical, but it really is genius, instead of rehashing the same story, which you claimed it did, and it doesn’t, it instead acknowledges how overused and overexposed Peter’s story is, and flips it’s predictable narrative on its head, it’s really well done.

Showing the plot of a movie has sometimes been a red flag. Granted it's not always bad, but there's a lot of bad animated movies that do this. And there were a lot of cool twists and reveals that could have been saved for the movie.

I'm going to guess what part of the plot is going to be.

Miguel O'hara has never been a villain in any of the comics or shows. The only reason he's kind of a rude jerk s because he comes from the future dystopia off 2099 where the world is ruled by ruthless mega-corporations (like in that game Cyberpunk 2077) and his dad was whacked by one of the mega-corporation CEOs. The only way he can achieve any kind of tangible results in his world to making it a better place is by being as ruthless and coldly efficient as someone like DC Comics Bruce Wayne Batman.

Miguel cares immensely about justice, morality and ethics since in his future dystopia world those 3 things are sadly not common. Also Miguel lost his own dad, he does not want anyone else to go through that kind of pain. He is NOT the type of guy who would tell someone else they need to lose their dad just to "fit the group' theme" or whatever.

I think Miguel just decided to tell Miles in advance "you need to let your dad perish by falling off a roof while fighting The Spot in order to have enough time to stop The Spot's [existence destruction device or whatever]" so that Miles does not end up as emotionally messed up as he would be if it just happened to him without any warning. However Miles tries to change fate and risks letting all existence get destroyed by The Spot, setting into motion the main conflict of the story.

I think Miguel's just going to be an anti-villain for part of the movie before later teaming up with Miles and the others.

Marvel already said the main villain for this movie was going to be The Spot. I think Miguel told Miles "my time viewing computer showed me you either need to stop the Spot [doing something that will destroy EVERY universe], or save your dad but let all existence die in the process". I think he honestly thinks that's true.

The main point of Spider-man as a fictional concept is not about self-fulfillment and self-realisation. That's more Ion Man or Captain America. The main point of Spider-man is someone selflessly using their great powers for the good of others and to try to always do the best thing when it comes to society as a whole even when that means immense stress, effort, time and self-sacrifice. "With great power comes great responsibility".

I think unfortunately Miguel is right, that Miles will be in a situation where he either has to stop The Spot or save his dad but let The Spot destroy all reality.

Maybe there'll be a twist where all the other Spider heroes come help Miles defeat the Spot AND save his dad. Perhaps Miguel is simply mistakenly. However the craziest twist (or un-twist) would be if Miles actually DOES have to decide to let his dad perish in order to defeat The Spot. It would be a great example of "with great power comes great responsibility" since Miles would have to do the sucky yet necessary responsible thing in that situation and let his dad perish in order to fulfill his moral obligation to use his power to save existence.

@Crystal Customs I don't want to be rude, so I am going to try to explain it: This isn't about realism, its about plausibility, and just because there is no person with superpowers in my backyard doesn't mean that everything else that has nothing to with super powers would change if it were the case. Trying to save one by risking everyone else is a terribly egotistical move that shouldn't be the "heroic" way, its the naive move that is likely to fail and kills many and that doesn't change just because you can shoot webs from your hands... In general: A fictional world is not necessarily realistic, but it should be intrinsically plausible. Fiction changes certain parameters about our world, but apart from those parameters nothing changes if it wants to stay believable to people who don't zone out when watching a movie. I would say the exact same critic about a "realistic" war movie in which someone risks all the other soldiers to save someone. This being a super hero movie doesn't change basic moral and logic.

@saikikusuo7937   How do I even explain this to a brainwashed person…

Look, I was born with a condition that causes me to have hand tremors and such, for real, I am telling the truth.  

My condition is in my brain.  I was born with it.  

But, it’s still a condition.  Which means that I wasn’t intended to have it—it means something went wrong.

And I have no right to force you to say that I don’t have hand tremors, for example.  

No one is denying human rights. 

Those of us with conditions do not have the right to demand others lie and say we don’t have a condition.  

Which is what people with sex dysphoria are demanding.  And that’s not a right.  

Lots of people have conditions.  Literally no one else with any other condition on earth is allowed to behave the way people with sex dysphoria are now behaving.  

For example, if I threatened to harm myself—do you know what would happen?  I would be sent straight to therapy, that’s what.  

No one has ever ever said to me that threatening to harm myself for any reason was acceptable behavior.  Yet, that is exactly what we’re telling people with sex dysphoria.  

I was never ever told that threatening self harm, threatening to harm others, or screaming, crying, etc. because I have a condition was acceptable behavior.  

No.  I was told the exact opposite.  I was told that if I needed help then I go to therapy.  And literally everyone on earth with any other condition knows this.  People with schizophrenia, people with autism, people with body identity integrity disorder (which is a very similar condition that’s also a brain one where from a young age they say a limb is foreign to them and they desire to be an amputee), etc.  

None of us are allowed to behave the way people with sex dysphoria behave.  

People with conditions, we do not have the right to force others to say what we want to hear—to lie about our condition.  I cannot throw someone in jail for telling the truth about my condition. 

And that’s not a human right.  Throwing people in jail if they say something I don’t want to hear isn’t a right.  No one has such a “right.”

@saikikusuo7937   And if you’re saying that children should be able to demand these surgeries and such—that’s also not something any child is allowed.  

Children do not have the ability to really comprehend the consequences of life altering decisions.  

And no child, even children without conditions, is allowed to make life altering decisions without a guardian’s consent.  

Some things children aren’t allowed to do even if an adult guardian approves.  Such as drinking alcohol. 

So no, it’s also not a human right for children to make life altering decisions.  No child is allowed—it’s not just those with sex dysphoria who aren’t allowed.  

Children do not have the ability to fully understand the consequences.

With respect I interpreted that last movie differently.

Just like Peter before him, Miles actions accidentally lead to another person dying. (Peter B Parker was his Uncle Ben.) As a result he is driven by guilt to make up for his mistake just like Peter was. That causes him to adopt the identity of a masked vigilante that does not resemble his private identity in anyway in order to make up for accidentally causing a man to die by helping save the lives of others. 

The Spider man entity was not even created by Miles, he is just inheriting Peter's legacy. He is even using one of Peter B Parker's old suits, not an original suit. All Miles did was paint one of the suits black and red to distinguish himself from the original Spider-man of his universe.


Miles' Spider-man story so far has been more about overcoming adversity and rising to the challenges life threw his way then about individuality or his sense of self in general.
